preFix.title: Re-gilding a decorative picture frame
preFix.subtitle: Apply gold transfer leaf to repair worn or bare sections of a gilded picture frame moulding
preFix.excerpt: How to repair the gilded surface of a decorative picture frame using gold transfer leaf, covering filling losses in the gesso ground, applying oil size as the adhesive, and laying transfer leaf so the repair blends with the original gilding.
preFix.sourceNotes: V&A Museum conservation notes on gilded frames (open access). Gilded Frame Conservators Handbook. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: Worn gilding on a picture frame is a cosmetic restoration, not a structural one. The goal is for the transfer leaf repair to be visually undetectable at normal viewing distance. The most important variable is the condition of the oil size when the leaf is applied: too wet and the leaf wrinkles. Too dry and it does not adhere. The knuckle-touch test is reliable.

Transfer gold leaf (22-carat for fine frames; Dutch metal imitation gold for painted or lower-value frames). Oil size, 3-hour drying time. Fine gesso paste or decorator's fine filler for filling any losses in the carved gesso ground. Fine sandpaper, 240 and 400 grit. Raw umber oil paint or acrylic tinted with raw umber for toning the repair after gilding. Small soft-hair paintbrush for applying size. Gilding brush or a soft mop brush for tamping the leaf.
Step 1, fill any losses in the gesso ground

Losses, areas where the carved or moulded gesso ground has chipped away, must be filled before gilding or the leaf will follow the uneven surface. Apply fine gesso or filler to each loss with the tip of the paintbrush. Building up in thin layers. Allow each layer to cure. Sand gently with 400-grit paper wrapped around a small foam block to match the surrounding profile. Fine carving detail can be worked back with a pointed tool before the filler fully sets. The filled surface should be smooth and level with the surrounding original gesso.

Apply a thin, even coat of 3-hour oil size to the repair area using the soft paintbrush. Include a few millimetres of undamaged gilded surface around each repair so the new leaf overlaps the existing gold and there is no bare size line at the edges. Allow the size to reach correct tack. About 3 hours at normal room temperature. Check by touching with a knuckle every 30 minutes from 2 hours onward.

Cut a piece of transfer leaf slightly larger than the repair area. Press the leaf tissue-side up over the sized area. Then press the tissue down gently with a soft brush or clean fingertip so the leaf contacts the size. Peel the tissue back slowly at a low angle; if any leaf lifts away with the tissue. Press the tissue back down and wait five minutes before peeling again. Use the soft brush to tamp down any loose edges and to press the leaf into any carved detail.
Step 4, tone the repair to match

Fresh gold leaf is bright and will read brighter than aged gilding. Allow the size to cure fully. Then apply a thin wash of raw umber oil or acrylic paint diluted to a glaze and wiped over the repair. The umber glaze tones down the brightness and fills the surface unevenness of the leaf. Blending the repair into the surrounding aged gilding. Build up the tone gradually, easier to add more than to remove.

attrs.fix: Apply a fresh coat of size over the failed area and wait for it to reach the correct tack before re-applying leaf. A new coat of size applied over a previously sized but over-dried surface needs only its own tack time.
attrs.cause: The size was too dry when the leaf was applied.
attrs.symptom: Leaf does not adhere and comes away with the tissue

attrs.fix: Allow the remaining sized areas to dry further. Wrinkled leaf that has adhered can sometimes be pressed flat with a soft brush and further tamping; wrinkled leaf that has not fully adhered can be lifted off by re-applying tissue paper and peeling, then re-applied once the size reaches the correct tack.
attrs.cause: The size was still too wet when the leaf was applied.
attrs.symptom: Leaf wrinkles or creases on the surface
